1:
"Now I Paul myself beseech you by the meekness and gentleness of Christ, who in presence am base among you, but being absent am bold toward you:" The witnesses note Paul's appeal by Christ's meekness, answering the charge of inconsistency. On "I Paul myself beseech you by the meekness and gentleness of Christ," the grammatical voices note the appeal. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, beginning his self-defense, appeals emphatically "I Paul myself" and entreats them "by the meekness and gentleness of Christ" — the very graces of Christ he is accused of lacking, and which he wishes to imitate.

On "in presence am base (lowly) among you, but being absent am bold," the witnesses note the charge. Barnes and Chrysostom note Paul cites the taunt of his opponents: that "in presence (face to face)" he is "base (lowly, humble, unimpressive)" but "being absent" (in his letters) is "bold" — they accused him of cowardice in person and bravado at a distance.

The Reformed Calvin notes the irony: Paul takes up their sneer and turns it; his bodily lowliness is no weakness but the meekness of Christ, and his boldness is the authority God gave him. The verse displays Paul's appeal by Christ's meekness, answering the charge of inconsistency.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul begins his self-defense, appealing "I Paul myself" and entreating "by the meekness and gentleness of Christ" — the very graces he is accused of lacking (Barnes, Robertson); citing the taunt that "in presence" he is "base (lowly, unimpressive)" but "being absent" (in letters) is "bold," accused of cowardice in person and bravado at a distance (Barnes, Chrysostom). Calvin notes Paul turns the sneer — his lowliness is Christ's meekness, his boldness God's authority. The verse displays Paul's appeal by Christ's meekness. The verse marks the appeal: by Christ's meekness. The verse displays the charge: base present, bold absent. The verse displays the irony: the sneer turned. The verse displays the defense: meekness and authority. The appeal displays Paul answering the charge of inconsistency by Christ's meekness. The verse displays the beseeching: I Paul. The verse displays the meekness: of Christ. The verse displays the charge: of inconsistency. Paul's appeal by Christ's meekness, answering the charge of inconsistency, opens his self-defense: entreating by the very gentleness of Christ he is accused of lacking, he takes up his opponents' taunt — that he is lowly in person but bold by letter — and turns it, his meekness being Christ's and his boldness God-given. The verse displays the apostle's defense of his ministry, asking that he need not be bold when present (v.2).
2:
"But I beseech you, that I may not be bold when I am present with that confidence, wherewith I think to be bold against some, which think of us as if we walked according to the flesh." The brief verse notes Paul's wish not to need boldness against those who charge him with worldliness. The grammatical voices note the entreaty. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "beseeches" them so to act that he "may not be bold when present" — that he may not have to use, against them, the stern authority he is prepared to use "against some."

On "some, which think of us as if we walked according to the flesh," the witnesses note the accusers. Barnes and Calvin note these "some" charge Paul with "walking according to the flesh" — acting from worldly, carnal motives, by craft, ambition, or self-interest; against such accusers Paul is ready to be bold.

The eastern Cyril and wesleyan-arminian Wesley note the distinction: Paul would rather come in gentleness, but against the obstinate slanderers who impugn his motives he will, if need be, exert his full apostolic authority. The verse displays Paul's wish not to need boldness against those who charge him with worldliness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "beseeches" them so to act that he "may not be bold when present," not having to use against them the stern authority he is prepared to use "against some" (Barnes, Robertson); these "some" charging him with "walking according to the flesh" — worldly, carnal motives, craft, or self-interest — against whom he is ready to be bold (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril and Wesley note he would rather come in gentleness but will exert his authority if need be. The verse displays his wish not to need boldness. The verse marks the entreaty: not to be bold present. The verse displays the readiness: against some. The verse displays the charge: walking after the flesh. The verse displays the distinction: gentleness preferred. The wish displays Paul preferring gentleness but ready to confront slanderers. The verse displays the beseeching: of Paul. The verse displays the boldness: held in reserve. The verse displays the accusers: of worldliness. Paul's wish not to need boldness against those who charge him with worldliness displays his preference for gentleness: he entreats them so to act that he need not exert, against them, the stern authority he holds ready against the slanderers who accuse him of walking by carnal motives. The verse displays the apostle's reluctant readiness for severity, grounded in the spiritual nature of his warfare (vv.3-6).
3:
"For though we walk in the flesh, we do not war after the flesh:" The brief verse notes that Paul, though living in the flesh, does not war by carnal means. The grammatical voices note the distinction. Barnes and Robertson note Paul concedes "we walk in the flesh" (live as men, in mortal bodies, subject to human weakness) but denies that "we war after the flesh" — he does not wage his spiritual warfare by carnal, worldly methods.

On the warfare, the witnesses note the figure. Barnes and Calvin note Paul takes up the metaphor of warfare for the ministry: though human and weak in body, the apostle's conflict for the gospel is not carried on by fleshly weapons (craft, eloquence, force, worldly power) but by spiritual means.

The Reformed Calvin notes the point: the minister of Christ, though a frail man, must not fight Satan and error with carnal weapons but with the spiritual armour God supplies; the warfare answers to the weapons. The verse displays that Paul does not war by carnal means.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul concedes "we walk in the flesh" (live as men, in mortal bodies, subject to weakness) but denies that "we war after the flesh," not waging his spiritual warfare by carnal, worldly methods (Barnes, Robertson); taking up the metaphor of warfare for the ministry, his conflict carried on not by fleshly weapons (craft, force, worldly power) but by spiritual means (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the warfare answers to the weapons. The verse displays not warring after the flesh. The verse marks the concession: we walk in the flesh. The verse displays the denial: not war after the flesh. The verse displays the figure: the spiritual warfare. The verse displays the means: not carnal. The distinction displays Paul's warfare carried on by spiritual, not carnal, means. The verse displays the walking: in the flesh. The verse displays the warring: not carnal. The verse displays the conflict: spiritual. That Paul, though living in the flesh, does not war by carnal means displays the spiritual nature of his ministry: though a frail mortal man, he does not wage the conflict of the gospel by worldly weapons of craft or force, but by the spiritual means God supplies. The verse displays the spiritual warfare of the apostle, armed with weapons mighty through God (v.4).
4:
"(For the weapons of our warfare are not carnal, but mighty through God to the pulling down of strong holds;)" The witnesses note the spiritual weapons mighty through God to pull down strongholds. On "the weapons of our warfare are not carnal," the grammatical voices note the means. Barnes and Robertson note Paul declares his "weapons" (the means of his ministry) are "not carnal" — not the eloquence, learning, wealth, force, or worldly power on which men rely — but spiritual: the truth of the gospel and the working of the Spirit.

On "mighty through God," the witnesses note the power. Barnes and Calvin note these weapons are "mighty (powerful) through God" — their efficacy is not in themselves but in God, who accompanies them with the power of his Spirit; the minister's strength is from God alone.

On "to the pulling down of strong holds," the witnesses note the conquest. Barnes and Ellicott note the military figure: these weapons are mighty "to the pulling down (demolishing) of strong holds" — the fortresses of error, sin, philosophy, idolatry, and pride that are entrenched against the gospel are battered down by spiritual means.

The witnesses note the confidence: there is nothing in the world so strongly fortified that God's spiritual weapons cannot overthrow it. The verse displays the spiritual weapons mighty through God to pull down strongholds.

The witnesses agree: Paul's "weapons" (the means of his ministry) are "not carnal" — not eloquence, learning, wealth, or worldly power — but spiritual (the truth of the gospel and the Spirit's working, Barnes, Robertson); "mighty through God," their efficacy not in themselves but in God who accompanies them with his Spirit (Barnes, Calvin); and "mighty to the pulling down (demolishing) of strong holds" — the fortresses of error, sin, philosophy, idolatry, and pride entrenched against the gospel (Barnes, Ellicott). Calvin notes nothing in the world is so fortified that God's weapons cannot overthrow it. The verse displays the spiritual weapons. The verse marks the means: not carnal. The verse displays the power: mighty through God. The verse displays the conquest: strongholds pulled down. The verse displays the confidence: all fortresses fall. The weapons display God's spiritual power demolishing the fortresses of evil. The verse displays the weapons: spiritual. The verse displays the might: of God. The verse displays the strongholds: pulled down. The spiritual weapons mighty through God to pull down strongholds display the true power of the ministry: not carnal means of eloquence or force, but the gospel and the Spirit, mighty through God to demolish the entrenched fortresses of error, sin, and pride that resist the truth. The verse displays the conquering power of spiritual weapons, carried into the captivity of every thought (v.5).
5:
"Casting down imaginations, and every high thing that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God, and bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ;" The witnesses note the conquest of every proud thought to the obedience of Christ. On "casting down imaginations" and "every high thing... against the knowledge of God," the grammatical voices note the targets. Barnes and Robertson note the spiritual weapons cast down "imaginations (reasonings, false philosophies, the devices of a wicked world)" and "every high thing (every lofty pretension, proud opinion, exalted obstacle) that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God" — all that proudly resists the gospel.

On "bringing into captivity every thought to the obedience of Christ," the witnesses note the surrender. Barnes and Calvin note the figure of conquest completed: "bringing into captivity (taking prisoner) every thought" — every faculty of the mind, every reasoning — "to the obedience of Christ"; the proud intellect is subdued and led captive to submit to Christ.

The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Reformed Calvin note the meaning: the human mind must give up its own wisdom, renounce the wisdom of the flesh, and surrender voluntarily, led captive by the Spirit into obedient faith. The verse displays the conquest of every proud thought to the obedience of Christ.

The witnesses agree: the spiritual weapons cast down "imaginations (reasonings, false philosophies)" and "every high thing (lofty pretension, proud opinion) that exalteth itself against the knowledge of God" — all that proudly resists the gospel (Barnes, Robertson); and complete the conquest by "bringing into captivity (taking prisoner) every thought (every faculty, every reasoning) to the obedience of Christ," the proud intellect subdued and led captive to submit to him (Barnes, Calvin). Wesley and Calvin note the mind must renounce its own wisdom and surrender voluntarily to obedient faith. The verse displays every thought brought captive to Christ. The verse marks the casting down: imaginations. The verse displays the high thing: against the knowledge of God. The verse displays the captivity: every thought. The verse displays the surrender: to Christ's obedience. The conquest displays the proud mind led captive to obey Christ. The verse displays the reasonings: cast down. The verse displays the pride: subdued. The verse displays the thought: captive. The conquest of every proud thought to the obedience of Christ displays the aim of the spiritual warfare: the weapons of the gospel cast down false reasonings and every proud thing that exalts itself against the knowledge of God, and bring every faculty of the mind captive — the proud intellect surrendering, led prisoner into obedient faith in Christ. The verse displays the captivity of the mind to Christ, with readiness to punish disobedience (v.6).
6:
"And having in a readiness to revenge all disobedience, when your obedience is fulfilled." The brief verse notes Paul's readiness to punish disobedience once the obedient are confirmed. The grammatical voices note the readiness. Barnes and Robertson note Paul stands "in a readiness to revenge (punish, avenge) all disobedience" — prepared, by his apostolic authority and discipline, to deal with the obstinate and rebellious who will not submit.

On "when your obedience is fulfilled," the witnesses note the order. Barnes and Calvin note the timing: Paul will exercise this discipline "when your obedience is fulfilled (completed)" — first the body of the church is to be brought to obedience, and then the few remaining rebels are to be dealt with; he distinguishes the obedient majority from the disobedient.

The Reformed Calvin notes the wisdom: Paul waits until the well-disposed have returned to obedience, that his severity may fall only on the obstinate, not on the whole church; discipline is exercised with discernment. The verse displays Paul's readiness to punish disobedience once the obedient are confirmed.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul stands "in a readiness to revenge (punish) all disobedience," prepared by his apostolic authority to deal with the obstinate and rebellious (Barnes, Robertson); the timing — "when your obedience is fulfilled," first the body of the church brought to obedience, then the remaining rebels dealt with, distinguishing the obedient from the disobedient (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes Paul waits that his severity fall only on the obstinate. The verse displays readiness to punish disobedience. The verse marks the readiness: to revenge disobedience. The verse displays the timing: obedience first fulfilled. The verse displays the distinction: obedient and rebellious. The verse displays the wisdom: discernment in discipline. The readiness displays Paul's discipline reserved for the obstinate. The verse displays the revenging: of disobedience. The verse displays the obedience: fulfilled first. The verse displays the discernment: in severity. Paul's readiness to punish disobedience once the obedient are confirmed displays the order of his discipline: he holds his apostolic authority ready to deal with the obstinate rebels, but only when the body of the church has returned to obedience — that his severity may fall on the disobedient alone, with discernment and not on the whole. The verse displays the just exercise of apostolic discipline, vindicating Paul's true claim to Christ (v.7).
7:
"Do ye look on things after the outward appearance? If any man trust to himself that he is Christ's, let him of himself think this again, that, as he is Christ's, even so are we Christ's." The brief verse notes Paul's claim to belong to Christ as truly as his rivals. The grammatical voices note the rebuke. Barnes and Robertson note Paul challenges their judging "after the outward appearance" — by surface, by the impressive bearing or claims of his rivals — rather than by reality (the clause read by some as a question, by some as a command, "Look at what is before your eyes").

On "if any man trust... that he is Christ's... so are we Christ's," the witnesses note the claim. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's point: if any (the rival teachers) confidently claims "that he is Christ's" (a true minister, belonging to Christ), let him also reckon that "as he is Christ's, even so are we Christ's" — Paul belongs to Christ as truly as any.

The eastern Chrysostom and puritan Henry note the dignity: Paul will not be reckoned inferior in belonging to Christ; whatever the rivals claim, he claims with at least equal right. The verse displays Paul's claim to belong to Christ as truly as his rivals.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul challenges their judging "after the outward appearance" — by surface and the impressive claims of his rivals — rather than reality (read as question or command, Barnes, Robertson); his point that if any rival claims "that he is Christ's," let him reckon that "as he is Christ's, even so are we Christ's," Paul belonging to Christ as truly as any (Barnes, Calvin). Chrysostom and Henry note Paul will not be reckoned inferior. The verse displays Paul's claim to Christ. The verse marks the rebuke: judging by appearance. The verse displays the claim: we are Christ's too. The verse displays the equality: at least equal right. The verse displays the dignity: not inferior. The claim displays Paul belonging to Christ as truly as his rivals. The verse displays the appearance: judged by. The verse displays the trust: of the rivals. The verse displays the belonging: Paul's also. Paul's claim to belong to Christ as truly as his rivals displays his answer to their surface judgments: they should not judge by outward appearance, for whatever the rival teachers claim in belonging to Christ, Paul belongs to him as truly — by at least equal right. The verse displays the apostle's equal standing in Christ, backed by the authority given him (v.8).
8:
"For though I should boast somewhat more of our authority, which the Lord hath given us for edification, and not for your destruction, I should not be ashamed:" The brief verse notes the authority given Paul for edification, not destruction. The grammatical voices note the boast. Barnes and Robertson note Paul says that even "if I should boast somewhat more of our authority" — claim still more of his apostolic power — he "should not be ashamed," for the claim is true and well-founded.

On "which the Lord hath given us for edification, and not for your destruction," the witnesses note the purpose of the power. Barnes and Calvin note the key qualification: the authority was given "for edification (building up)" — to build the church — "and not for destruction (pulling down)"; Paul's power, unlike a tyrant's, is for their good, not their harm.

The Reformed Calvin notes the comfort: the apostle's authority, however real and severe it may sometimes be, always aims at the church's edification; even his discipline is meant to save, not to ruin. The verse displays the authority given Paul for edification, not destruction.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul says even "if I should boast somewhat more of our authority," claiming still more of his apostolic power, he "should not be ashamed," the claim being true (Barnes, Robertson); the authority given "for edification (building up), not for destruction (pulling down)," Paul's power being for their good, not their harm (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes the authority always aims at edification. The verse displays authority for edification. The verse marks the boast: of authority. The verse displays the source: the Lord gave it. The verse displays the purpose: edification. The verse displays the limit: not destruction. The authority displays Paul's power aimed at building up the church. The verse displays the boasting: justified. The verse displays the edification: its end. The verse displays the destruction: not its aim. The authority given Paul for edification, not destruction, displays the nature of his apostolic power: he could boast still more of it without shame, for the Lord gave it to build up the church and not to tear it down — his authority, even in severity, aiming always at their good. The verse displays the edifying purpose of his power, lest he seem to terrify by letters (v.9).
9:
"That I may not seem as if I would terrify you by letters." The brief verse notes Paul's care not to seem to frighten them merely by letters. The grammatical voices note the disclaimer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul adds this lest, in asserting his authority, he "seem as if I would terrify (frighten) you by letters" — as though his stern letters were empty threats meant only to scare, with no real power behind them.

On the charge, the witnesses note the accusation answered. Barnes and Wesley note Paul takes up his opponents' taunt (stated in v.10): that his letters were weighty and frightening but his presence weak; he answers that his letters are no mere bluster.

The grammatical-historical note the consistency: Paul will be in deed, when present, what he is in word, when absent; his letters are not idle threats. The verse displays Paul's care not to seem to frighten them merely by letters.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul adds this lest, asserting his authority, he "seem as if I would terrify you by letters" — as though his stern letters were empty threats to scare, with no real power (Barnes, Robertson); taking up the taunt (v.10) that his letters were weighty but his presence weak, answering that they are no bluster (Barnes, Wesley). The grammatical-historical note he will be in deed what he is in word. The verse displays not terrifying by letters. The verse marks the disclaimer: not to terrify by letters. The verse displays the charge: empty threats. The verse displays the answer: no bluster. The verse displays the consistency: word and deed. The disclaimer displays Paul's letters backed by real authority. The verse displays the terrifying: disclaimed. The verse displays the letters: not idle. The verse displays the power: real. Paul's care not to seem to frighten them merely by letters displays his answer to the taunt that his letters were empty bluster: he asserts his authority not to scare with idle threats but because his letters carry real power, which he will make good in deed when present. The verse displays the apostle's consistency of word and deed, against the sneer at his presence (vv.10-11).
10:
"For his letters, say they, are weighty and powerful; but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible." The brief verse notes the opponents' taunt at Paul's presence and speech. The grammatical voices note the sneer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes his critics directly: "his letters, say they, are weighty (impressive) and powerful (forceful)" — they admit the strength of his writing — "but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible (of no account)."

On the taunt, the witnesses note its substance. Barnes and Wesley note the opponents disparaged Paul's personal appearance (perhaps unimpressive or infirm) and his manner of speaking (lacking the polished rhetoric prized by the Greeks); they used this to undermine his authority.

The grammatical-historical note the irony: the critics concede the power of his letters while despising the man — but the same authority that speaks in the letters belongs to the man, as Paul will insist. The verse displays the opponents' taunt at Paul's presence and speech.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes his critics — "his letters, say they, are weighty (impressive) and powerful," admitting the strength of his writing, "but his bodily presence is weak, and his speech contemptible (of no account)" (Barnes, Robertson); they disparaged his unimpressive appearance and his manner of speaking, lacking polished rhetoric, to undermine his authority (Barnes, Wesley). The grammatical-historical note the irony: they concede the letters while despising the man. The verse displays the opponents' taunt. The verse marks the concession: weighty letters. The verse displays the sneer: weak presence. The verse displays the disparagement: contemptible speech. The verse displays the irony: power conceded. The taunt displays the critics despising the man while fearing his letters. The verse displays the letters: powerful. The verse displays the presence: weak. The verse displays the speech: despised. The opponents' taunt at Paul's presence and speech displays the slander he answers: his critics conceded that his letters were weighty and forceful but despised his bodily presence as weak and his speech as contemptible — using his lack of imposing appearance and polished rhetoric to undermine his authority. The verse displays the sneer Paul rebuts by his consistency (v.11).
11:
"Let such an one think this, that, such as we are in word by letters when we are absent, such will we be also in deed when we are present." The brief verse notes Paul's warning that he will be in deed what he is in word. The grammatical voices note the answer. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's reply to the taunt: "let such an one (the critic) think this" — let him be assured — that "such as we are in word by letters when absent, such will we be also in deed when present"; there is no gap between his letters and his action.

On the consistency, the witnesses note the warning. Barnes and Robertson note this is a quiet but firm warning: the boldness and authority of his letters are not empty; when he comes, he will act with the same firmness — the critics should not presume on his supposed weakness in person.

The grammatical-historical note the point: Paul's character is consistent; his words and his deeds agree, and his presence will prove as weighty as his letters. The verse displays Paul's warning that he will be in deed what he is in word.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul replies to the taunt — "let such an one think this," that "such as we are in word by letters when absent, such will we be also in deed when present," no gap between his letters and his action (Barnes, Robertson); a quiet but firm warning that the boldness of his letters is not empty, and the critics should not presume on his supposed weakness in person (Barnes, Robertson). The grammatical-historical note his words and deeds agree. The verse displays word matched by deed. The verse marks the warning: think this. The verse displays the consistency: word and deed. The verse displays the firmness: not empty. The verse displays the caution: presume not. The warning displays Paul's presence proving as weighty as his letters. The verse displays the word: by letters. The verse displays the deed: when present. The verse displays the agreement: of both. Paul's warning that he will be in deed what he is in word displays his firm answer to the sneer: the critics should be assured that the boldness and authority of his letters are no empty bluster — when he comes in person, he will act with the very same firmness, his presence proving as weighty as his words. The verse displays the apostle's consistency, leading into his measured boasting (v.12).
12:
"For we dare not make ourselves of the number, or compare ourselves with some that commend themselves: but they measuring themselves by themselves, and comparing themselves among themselves, are not wise." The witnesses note the folly of the self-commenders who measure themselves by themselves. On "we dare not... compare ourselves with some that commend themselves," the grammatical voices note the irony. Barnes and Robertson note Paul says, with biting irony, that he "dares not" rank or compare himself with those "that commend themselves" — the rival teachers whose only credential is their own self-praise.

On "measuring themselves by themselves, and comparing themselves among themselves," the witnesses note the folly. Barnes and Calvin note the self-commenders' error: they take themselves (and each other) as the standard — "measuring themselves by themselves" — instead of by God's measure; a closed circle of mutual flattery.

On "are not wise," the puritan Henry and grammatical-historical note the verdict: such self-comparison "is not wise" (is foolish) — it produces only conceit and self-deception, for the true standard is not oneself but God's calling and approval. The verse displays the folly of the self-commenders.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul says with irony that he "dares not" rank himself with those "that commend themselves," whose only credential is self-praise (Barnes, Robertson); their error being to take themselves and each other as the standard — "measuring themselves by themselves" — a closed circle of mutual flattery instead of God's measure (Barnes, Calvin); and such self-comparison "is not wise" (foolish), producing only conceit (Henry). The verse displays the folly of self-commendation. The verse marks the irony: dare not compare. The verse displays the credential: self-praise. The verse displays the error: their own standard. The verse displays the verdict: not wise. The folly displays the self-commenders in a closed circle of flattery. The verse displays the commending: of themselves. The verse displays the measuring: by themselves. The verse displays the folly: of it. The folly of the self-commenders who measure themselves by themselves displays Paul's rebuke of his rivals: he will not rank himself among those whose only credential is self-praise, for to take oneself and one's peers as the standard, rather than God's measure, is not wisdom but self-deceiving conceit. The verse displays the vanity of self-comparison, against which Paul boasts only within God's measure (v.13).
13:
"But we will not boast of things without our measure, but according to the measure of the rule which God hath distributed to us, a measure to reach even unto you." The brief verse notes Paul boasting only within the measure God assigned him. The grammatical voices note the limit. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, unlike the self-commenders, "will not boast of things without (beyond) our measure" — he will not claim what is not his — but only "according to the measure of the rule (kanon, the assigned province, the limit) which God hath distributed (apportioned) to us."

On "a measure to reach even unto you," the witnesses note Corinth's inclusion. Barnes and Calvin note that the field God assigned Paul "reaches even unto you" — Corinth fell within his God-given sphere; he was their apostle by divine appointment, not an intruder.

The Reformed Calvin notes the principle: every minister should keep within the bounds of his calling, boasting only of what God has actually given him to do; Paul's boast is measured by God's apportionment. The verse displays Paul boasting only within the measure God assigned him.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "will not boast of things without (beyond) our measure," not claiming what is not his, but only "according to the measure of the rule (the assigned province) which God hath distributed to us" (Barnes, Robertson); the field God assigned him "reaching even unto you," Corinth falling within his God-given sphere, so he was their apostle by appointment, not an intruder (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes every minister should keep within his calling. The verse displays boasting within God's measure. The verse marks the limit: not beyond measure. The verse displays the rule: God's apportionment. The verse displays the sphere: reaching to Corinth. The verse displays the principle: keep within calling. The boasting displays Paul confined to his God-given field. The verse displays the measure: assigned. The verse displays the rule: God's. The verse displays the reach: to Corinth. Paul boasting only within the measure God assigned him displays the modesty of his self-defense: unlike the self-commenders, he claims nothing beyond his God-apportioned field — and that field reaches even to Corinth, which was his by divine appointment, not by intrusion. The verse displays the bounded boasting of the apostle, who did not overreach (v.14).
14:
"For we stretch not ourselves beyond our measure, as though we reached not unto you: for we are come as far as to you also in preaching the gospel of Christ:" The brief verse notes Paul not overreaching, for he truly reached Corinth with the gospel. The grammatical voices note the claim. Barnes and Robertson note Paul "stretches not himself beyond his measure (overextends not his claim) as though he reached not unto you" — he is not overreaching when he claims Corinth, for he genuinely "came as far as to you also in preaching the gospel of Christ."

On "we are come as far as to you," the witnesses note the fact. Barnes and Calvin note the decisive point: Paul was the very one who first brought the gospel to Corinth (Acts 18); he was their founding apostle, so his authority over them is no presumption but his rightful sphere.

On "in preaching the gospel of Christ," the eastern Cyril notes the ground: it was by actually preaching Christ to them that Paul's claim was established — his apostleship among them is proved by their very existence as a church. The verse displays Paul not overreaching, for he truly reached Corinth.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul "stretches not himself beyond his measure as though he reached not unto you," not overreaching when he claims Corinth, for he genuinely "came as far as to you also in preaching the gospel" (Barnes, Robertson); the decisive point being that Paul first brought the gospel to Corinth (Acts 18), their founding apostle, so his authority is no presumption (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril notes his apostleship is proved by their existence as a church. The verse displays Paul truly reaching Corinth. The verse marks the claim: not overreaching. The verse displays the fact: came to you. The verse displays the founding: by the gospel. The verse displays the ground: their existence. The claim displays Paul's rightful authority as their founding apostle. The verse displays the reaching: to Corinth. The verse displays the preaching: of the gospel. The verse displays the right: established. Paul not overreaching, for he truly reached Corinth with the gospel, displays the solid ground of his claim: he does not overextend in asserting authority over Corinth, since he was the very one who first brought them the gospel — their founding apostle, whose right over them is established by their own existence as a church. The verse displays the apostle's rightful sphere, with hope of further enlargement (v.15).
15:
"Not boasting of things without our measure, that is, of other men's labours; but having hope, when your faith is increased, that we shall be enlarged by you according to our rule abundantly," The brief verse notes Paul not boasting in others' labours, but hoping for enlarged ministry. The grammatical voices note the contrast. Barnes and Robertson note Paul, unlike his rivals, is "not boasting of things without our measure, that is, of other men's labours" — he does not take credit for work done by others (as the intruders did, building on his foundation).

On "when your faith is increased... we shall be enlarged by you," the witnesses note the hope. Barnes and Calvin note Paul's expectation: "when your faith is increased" (when the Corinthians are confirmed and grown), he hopes to be "enlarged by you" — to have his sphere of labour extended through them, "according to our rule (his assigned measure) abundantly."

The eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the aim: Paul hopes that, with Corinth established, he may press the gospel into the regions beyond, using Corinth as a base — not poaching others' fields but breaking new ground. The verse displays Paul not boasting in others' labours, but hoping for enlarged ministry.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, unlike his rivals, is "not boasting of other men's labours," not taking credit for work done by others (Barnes, Robertson); his hope that "when your faith is increased" he may be "enlarged by you," his sphere extended through them "according to our rule abundantly" (Barnes, Calvin); aiming, with Corinth established, to press into the regions beyond (Cyril). The verse displays not boasting in others' labours. The verse marks the contrast: not others' labours. The verse displays the hope: enlarged by you. The verse displays the condition: faith increased. The verse displays the aim: regions beyond. The hope displays Paul extending his own field, not seizing another's. The verse displays the boasting: not in others. The verse displays the enlargement: hoped. The verse displays the rule: his measure. Paul not boasting in others' labours, but hoping for enlarged ministry, displays the integrity of his ambition: he takes no credit for work done by others, but hopes that as the Corinthians' faith grows, his own sphere may be extended through them — that he may carry the gospel, within his rightful measure, into new regions beyond. The verse displays the apostle's forward hope, to preach where none had gone (v.16).
16:
"To preach the gospel in the regions beyond you, and not to boast in another man's line of things made ready to our hand." The brief verse notes Paul's aim to preach in untouched regions, not in another's field. The grammatical voices note the ambition. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's forward purpose: "to preach the gospel in the regions beyond you" — to carry it into lands not yet evangelized, pressing into new territory.

On "not to boast in another man's line of things made ready to our hand," the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note Paul will "not boast in another man's line (sphere) of things made ready to our hand" — he will not glory in work already done by others, building on another's foundation; that was the intruders' way, not his.

The eastern Cyril and grammatical-historical note the principle: Paul's holy ambition was to be a pioneer, breaking new ground for Christ where no one had labored (Romans 15:20) — not to reap where another had sown. The verse displays Paul's aim to preach in untouched regions, not in another's field.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's forward purpose "to preach the gospel in the regions beyond you," carrying it into unevangelized lands (Barnes, Robertson); and "not to boast in another man's line of things made ready to our hand," not glorying in others' completed work or building on another's foundation, as the intruders did (Barnes, Calvin). Cyril notes Paul's ambition to be a pioneer (Romans 15:20). The verse displays preaching in untouched regions. The verse marks the aim: regions beyond. The verse displays the contrast: not another's field. The verse displays the principle: pioneer ground. The verse displays the rebuke: of the intruders. The aim displays Paul breaking new ground, not reaping another's. The verse displays the preaching: in new lands. The verse displays the boasting: not in another's. The verse displays the pioneer: Paul. Paul's aim to preach in untouched regions, not in another's field, displays the integrity of his holy ambition: he sought to carry the gospel into lands not yet reached, breaking new ground for Christ — never glorying in another's completed work, as his rivals did who built on his foundation. The verse displays the pioneer spirit of the apostle, whose only true boast is in the Lord (v.17).
17:
"But he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord." The brief verse notes the rule that all glorying must be in the Lord. The grammatical voices note the citation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul quotes Jeremiah 9:24 (as in 1 Corinthians 1:31): "he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord" — all boasting is to be directed to the Lord, not to self or human attainment.

On the principle, the witnesses note the contrast. Barnes and Calvin note this answers the self-commenders: their glorying was in themselves, their own measure and labors; but the only legitimate glorying is in the Lord — in what God has done and given, ascribing all to him.

The Reformed Calvin notes the foundation: all that the minister is and does is from God; therefore the only true boast is in the Lord, who alone gives the calling, the gifts, and the success. The verse displays the rule that all glorying must be in the Lord.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul quotes Jeremiah 9:24 (as in 1 Corinthians 1:31) — "he that glorieth, let him glory in the Lord," all boasting directed to the Lord, not self (Barnes, Robertson); this answering the self-commenders, whose glorying was in themselves, while the only legitimate glorying is in the Lord, ascribing all to him (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes all the minister is and does is from God. The verse displays glorying in the Lord. The verse marks the citation: Jeremiah 9. The verse displays the rule: glory in the Lord. The verse displays the contrast: not in self. The verse displays the foundation: all from God. The rule displays all true boasting directed to the Lord. The verse displays the glorying: in the Lord. The verse displays the self: excluded. The verse displays the ascription: to God. The rule that all glorying must be in the Lord displays the principle that governs Paul's whole defense: against the self-commenders who glory in themselves, the only legitimate boast is in the Lord — for all that a minister is and does is God's gift, and to him alone the glory is due. The verse displays the proper object of all boasting, sealed by God's own commendation (v.18).
18:
"For not he that commendeth himself is approved, but whom the Lord commendeth." The witnesses note that God's commendation, not self-commendation, is what approves. On "not he that commendeth himself is approved," the grammatical voices note the rejection. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's closing principle: "not he that commendeth himself is approved" — self-praise proves nothing; the rival teachers' self-commendation does not make them approved ministers.

On "but whom the Lord commendeth," the witnesses note the true approval. Barnes and Calvin note the standard: "whom the Lord commendeth" is the one truly approved — God's commendation (shown by his calling, his gifts, and the fruit of his blessing on the ministry) is the only valid credential.

The Reformed Calvin notes the application: Paul's true commendation lay in the seal God set on his ministry (the Corinthians themselves, ch. 3), not in self-assertion; the test of a true minister is God's approval, not his own claims. The verse displays that God's commendation, not self-commendation, is what approves.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul's closing principle — "not he that commendeth himself is approved," self-praise proving nothing, the rivals' self-commendation not making them approved (Barnes, Robertson); the standard being "whom the Lord commendeth," God's commendation (shown by his calling, gifts, and the fruit of his blessing) the only valid credential (Barnes, Calvin). Calvin notes Paul's true commendation lay in the seal God set on his ministry. The verse displays God's commendation as the true approval. The verse marks the rejection: self-commendation. The verse displays the standard: the Lord's commendation. The verse displays the credential: God's approval. The verse displays the test: of a true minister. The principle displays God's approval, not self-praise, as the true credential. The verse displays the self-commender: not approved. The verse displays the Lord: who commends. The verse displays the approval: from God. That God's commendation, not self-commendation, is what approves displays the principle that crowns Paul's defense: no minister is approved by praising himself, as the rivals did, but only the one whom the Lord commends — by his calling, his gifts, and the fruit of his blessing — God's approval being the only valid credential. The verse displays the true test of the minister, closing Paul's vindication of his apostolic authority.
